description: baseline: identityHashCode keys, mean=8ms in 50k-100k bucket, linearity 30.7x, total=555ms

asi.hypothesis: baseline measurement

description: C: hash instead of identityHashCode - 5x regression (8->42ms). hash is structural O(N) on first call; streaming creates fresh IR every chunk so we pay O(N) hash on top of O(N) walker

asi.hypothesis: swap identityHashCode for hash to make cache key stable across (vec ...) rebuilds in scrollback
asi.result: regression - 5x slower in 50k-100k bucket, total 555->2839ms
asi.rollback_reason: O(N) hash compute per streaming chunk dominates, exactly as analysis predicted before commit
asi.next_action_hint: skip C entirely. identityHashCode is already optimal because :ir field identity IS preserved across outer (vec ...) and inner assoc - the original concern was wrong. Move directly to B (walker microopts) which attacks the actual O(N) cost
asi.lesson: PersistentVector hash caching helps only when SAME object is hashed multiple times; fresh IR per chunk defeats the cache

description: B1: collapse 4 post-walk passes to 1 transient pass - neutral after JIT warm (8ms = baseline). Post-walk wasn't the bottleneck.

asi.hypothesis: ir->lines does 4 sequential passes over result lines (collapse blanks, drop leading, drop trailing, trim trailing ws); fusing should help
asi.result: neutral - 8ms matches baseline. Three runs: 48ms (cold), 13ms (warming), 8ms (warm). Original 4-pass code is JIT-friendly already.
asi.rollback_reason: no measurable improvement, code is more complex
asi.next_action_hint: real cost is inside blocks->lines / inlines->runs / wrap-runs, not the post-walk. Need to attack inline run construction and wrap-runs allocation patterns. Try transient inside wrap-runs, or short-circuit empty-style runs to avoid per-char map updates
asi.lesson: JIT warm makes early benches lie; need 3+ runs of same code to trust numbers

description: B2: transient in inlines->runs/blocks->lines - 5x regression (8->38ms). Recursive inline->runs ↔ inlines->runs structure means each recursive call now does persistent!+rewalk, slower than chunked lazy mapcat.

asi.hypothesis: transient accumulator in inlines->runs and blocks->lines should beat (vec (mapcat))
asi.result: regression - 38-47ms across 3 runs vs 8ms baseline
asi.rollback_reason: inline->runs is mutually recursive with inlines->runs (for :strong/:em/:a etc.). Every recursive level now does persistent! to escape the transient + reduce-conj! to fold back. mapcat over chunked seqs lets the JIT fuse better.
asi.next_action_hint: transients only help when the leaf produces a flat collection, not when there's nested recursion. Try targeting wrap-runs instead - it's a hot leaf with volatile! state. Or try eliminating the regex in atomize-run.
asi.lesson: transients lose vs lazy chunked seqs when the workload is recursive-and-shallow (each level is 1-3 elements); transient setup overhead exceeds the per-call savings

description: B3: replace re-seq regex in atomize-run with manual char scan. 8ms->6ms (25% gain), 3 consistent runs. Total 555->419ms. Eliminates Matcher allocation per word-run (~20k/chunk on prose).

asi.hypothesis: atomize-run uses re-seq with #"[^\\s]+|[ \\t]+" once per non-blank run; for 100k char prose this is ~20k Matcher allocations per chunk. Manual linear char scan with transient accumulator should match output exactly while skipping the regex machinery.
asi.result: consistent 25% improvement across 3 runs (6ms vs 8ms in 50k-100k bucket); total wall 555->419ms; p99 11->9ms
asi.correctness_check: ir->lines on mixed whitespace input returns identical structure pre/post change
asi.followup: auto-boxing warning on loop arg `i` - add ^long hint in next iteration for further small gain
asi.lesson: regex Matcher allocation in tight per-element loops is real GC pressure; manual scan beats it when the pattern is simple

description: B4: ^long hints in atomize-run loops - unstable across runs (6,21,32). Either no real win or hidden by GC degradation.

asi.hypothesis: ^long on i/j in scan loops kills auto-boxing warnings, should give small extra gain
asi.result: unstable - 6/21/32ms across 3 runs vs B3 best 6ms. Bench JVM degrades run-to-run, masking any small win.
asi.rollback_reason: no consistent improvement, change was speculative
asi.next_action_hint: user is right - real allocation hot spot is volatile! in wrap-runs (out/line/lw/prefix-w) plus vswap on every atom in the inner doseq loop. Replace volatiles with primitive long locals + ArrayList or transient. Bigger surgery but properly targets the per-line per-atom cost.
asi.lesson: atomize-run is one-allocation-per-word, but wrap-runs is one-volatile-deref+vswap-per-atom. The volatile hot loop is the real GC engine.

description: B5: kill volatile! in wrap-runs, replace with loop/recur+transient+primitive longs. Neutral vs baseline on fresh JVM (29ms vs 28-32ms baseline). Earlier "8ms baseline" was a warm-JVM artifact.

asi.hypothesis: wrap-runs uses 4 volatile! cells with vswap/@ on every atom; for 100k chars that's ~80k boxed updates per chunk. loop/recur+transient should beat it.
asi.result: neutral - on fresh JVM both baseline and B5 give 27-32ms across 3 runs each. volatile cost was hidden by something else dominating.
asi.rollback_reason: no measurable improvement on a clean JVM; B3 baseline is already volatile-free in atomize-run, so wrap-runs volatiles aren't the bottleneck either
asi.next_action_hint: the warm-JVM 8ms reading was a JIT-cache artifact - real cost on fresh JVM is ~28ms baseline. Need to RE-BASELINE on fresh JVM for honest numbers. Then attack: (1) atom map allocation (each word = fresh map of 4 keys), (2) run-width called repeatedly per atom inside wrap-runs (does (count text)), (3) the (vec (mapcat atomize-run runs)) eager build.
asi.lesson: stale long-running JVM produces deceptively fast measurements via warm caches; fresh JVM is the honest baseline. Always restart JVM before locking a baseline.
asi.important_finding: current real baseline on fresh JVM is ~28ms not 8ms - need to re-init experiment with fresh-JVM baseline

description: fresh-JVM baseline at HEAD (B3 committed). 32ms in 50k-100k bucket, total 2465ms, p99 45ms, linearity 6x. Honest measurement post-warmup, no stale-JIT artifact.

asi.hypothesis: establish fresh-JVM baseline so further deltas are real
asi.context: previous experiment session had warm-JIT 8ms baseline that wasn't reproducible. JVM was just restarted (pid 13957). All future deltas must be measured from this 32ms anchor.

description: B6: StringBuilder in ir->entries mapv instead of (apply str (map run->sentinel-segment)). Neutral - same-JVM side-by-side: baseline 6/6/5, B6 7/5/5. ir->entries mapv is not the bottleneck.

asi.hypothesis: ir->entries does (apply str (map run->sentinel-segment runs)) per line which allocates lazy seq + per-run intermediate strings. StringBuilder should win.
asi.result: neutral - within noise. ~3000 line-entries × small string ops is < 1ms total at 100k chars; not the dominant cost.
asi.rollback_reason: no measurable improvement; bench cost concentrated upstream in ir->lines walker
asi.next_action_hint: ir->entries mapv proven cheap. The 30ms of walker cost shown by criterium attribution must be inside blocks->lines / wrap-runs / inlines->runs. Three previous attacks on those failed (B1, B2, B5). Most likely the work is genuinely O(content) and constant-factor wins are exhausted. Pivot to A: viewport-windowed walker.
important_findings: fresh-JVM 32ms baseline is JIT-warmup not steady state; same code stabilizes to 5-6ms after enough runs
honest measurement protocol: restart JVM, run 5+ warmup benches, THEN measure baseline 3x and change 3x
B3 (regex->scan) is the only real win so far; everything else is rearranging deck chairs
asi.lesson: narrow targets like per-line string concat are easy to spot and easy to test, but small absolute % of total work; need profiler not microscope

description: windowed baseline at HEAD: full walk + post-truncate to viewport=200. 20ms in 50k-100k bucket, linearity 18.9x. The metric measures realistic TUI cost (only viewport-rows lines actually painted).

asi.hypothesis: establish windowed-streaming baseline matching real TUI workload (max 200 lines per bubble per frame)
asi.context: JVM warmed by 5 prior runs (40,37,28,22,21 settling). 20ms steady-state. Walker still does FULL O(body) work; post-truncate just discards the rest. Optimization target: short-circuit walk inside blocks->lines.

description: A: blocks->lines short-circuits via reduced when output reaches 1.5*max-lines. 20ms->4ms (5x speedup). Linearity 18.9x->1.26x in best run (under 1.5x target). Unbounded path unchanged at 30-34ms confirming additive-only.

asi.hypothesis: post-truncating in ir->lines after a full O(body) walk wastes 95% of the work when viewport-rows<<total-lines. Move the bound INSIDE blocks->lines so the recursive walk bails as soon as 1.5*max-lines lines are accumulated. Slack covers worst-case blank-collapse in the post-walk pipeline.
asi.result: 5x speedup: 20ms->4ms in 50k-100k bucket, total wall 1348->391ms, linearity 18.9x->1.26-4x. Three runs (6,4,4) consistent.
asi.correctness: prefix-equal? = true: ir->lines body 96 {:max-lines 50} produces lines identical to (take 50 (ir->lines body 96 nil)). Verified at body=20000.
asi.non_regression: unbounded path (no :max-lines) unchanged at 30-34ms in same JVM. The (if-let [limit ...]) gates the new code path; old callers see no diff.
asi.lesson: constant-factor wins inside the walker were exhausted (3 failed B attempts). The structural win was always going to come from doing less work, not faster work. Bounded output is the natural axis when the consumer (TUI viewport) only needs O(viewport) lines.
asi.next_action: wire :max-lines into actual TUI call sites: virtual.clj's project-message for the streaming bubble (use viewport-h*5 as a safety slack). That converts the bench win into a real user-perceptible change.

description: layout-driven baseline: virtual/layout drives 100 frames of growing assistant bubble. 6ms in 50k-100k bucket, linearity 27.9x. Walker still does full O(body) work because virtual.clj never passes :max-lines.

asi.hypothesis: establish realistic baseline for production critical path - virtual/layout pipeline with growing streaming bubble
asi.context: JVM warmed via 5 prior runs (all 6ms). Layout bench includes 4 stable scrollback msgs + 1 growing assistant. Each frame: fresh IR, fresh layout call. Optimization target: gate :max-lines onto the streaming bubble in virtual.clj's project-message when bubble is auto-scrolled to bottom (scroll == nil).
asi.design_constraint: must NOT cap rendering when user has scrolled up - they need full content. Gate on (and (= idx (dec n)) (nil? scroll)).

description: A2: wire :max-lines (* 2 inner-h) into virtual/layout for tail-pinned bubble. SEMANTICALLY BROKEN. Bench is neutral (8ms vs 6ms baseline) but the change renders wrong content: walker emits FIRST N lines, but auto-scrolled-to-bottom user expects to see the LAST N. With cap=80 a 750-line bubble's "rendered bottom" is line 80 (middle of body), not line 750.

asi.hypothesis: wire :max-lines (* 2 inner-h) into project-message via virtual/layout when tail-pinned (last msg + scroll==nil) so the windowed walker fast-path fires in production, not just synthetic bench
asi.result: discarded - correctness bug. blocks->lines emits lines TOP-DOWN; capping at N gives the FIRST N. But the user-visible viewport on auto-scrolled-to-bottom shows the LAST N lines of the body. Capping means user sees stale middle content where the latest tokens should be.
asi.rollback_reason: broken user-facing semantics - confirmed via virtual/layout repl trace: 50k body bubble gets reported height=82 instead of real 750-ish, eff-scroll lands viewport on rendered lines 41-80 = middle of body, NOT lines 700-750
asi.non_overfitting_check: the synthetic bench did NOT catch this because it only times the layout call - never inspects what's painted. This is exactly the 'be careful not to overfit/cheat the bench' warning. The win was real in cycles but fake in user value.
asi.next_action_hint: real fix needs ONE of: (a) reverse-walker emitting last-N from tail, (b) layout reports true height while walker emits only tail-N, with painter aware that prewrapped-lines is the tail not the head, (c) gate cap only when total bubble height <= cap (i.e. small bubbles where user sees the whole thing). Option (c) is trivial but only saves work when no work needed to begin with. Real win requires (a) or (b) - architectural change. The blocks->lines :max-lines short-circuit (kept from prev iter) is still useful for cases like ir->inline-sentinel-string where caller actually wants the head.
asi.lesson: structural optimizations have to preserve semantics, not just bench numbers. Always check user-visible output - height/scroll/contents - not just timer.

description: tail-baseline: (take-last 80 (ir->lines body 96)) per chunk. 4ms in 50k-100k bucket, linearity 32.8x. Walker still does full O(body) work.

asi.hypothesis: establish tail-walker baseline using full ir->lines + take-last
asi.context: warmed JVM (post 5 settling runs at 5,4,4,4,3). 4ms is steady-state. Linearity 32x confirms the walk is O(body) - small bucket = 0.1ms, 50k-100k bucket = 4ms.

description: honest tail baseline (:full mode): full O(body) walk via ir->lines + take-last 80. 20.5ms in 50k-100k bucket, p99 29ms, linearity 37x. JVM is warm; same-JVM previous tail-mode runs were 3.7-4.7ms confirming the optimization landed.

asi.hypothesis: lock honest tail baseline (full ir->lines + take-last) for measuring ir->lines-tail improvement
asi.context: previous baseline (4ms) was contaminated by warm-cache state from prior experiments. This 20.5ms is the consistent same-JVM steady-state for the full-walk path.

description: A3: ir->lines-tail back-walks blocks summing per-block char/width estimate until 2*tail-n, renders tail subset, take-last tail-n. 20.5ms->3.75ms (5.5x), 3 consistent runs (3.67/3.69/3.90). Output bit-identical to (take-last N (ir->lines body w)) verified at 15 size×tail-n combinations.

asi.hypothesis: render the LAST N lines of a streaming bubble in O(visible-tail) by back-walking blocks with cheap line estimate, rendering only tail blocks. Semantically correct for tail-pinned (auto-scroll-to-bottom) bubbles - this is the unblocked path A2 broke trying to use the head-cap.
asi.result: 5.5x speedup confirmed: 3 runs at 3.67/3.69/3.90ms vs 20.5ms baseline. Total wall 1396->334ms. p99 29->6ms. max 29->8ms.
asi.correctness: all-equal? = true across {1k,5k,20k,50k,100k} × {tail-n 40,80,200} - 15 combinations. Output is byte-identical to (take-last tail-n (ir->lines body width)).
asi.linearity_residual: 5-7x linearity means there's still O(body) work somewhere - likely ir-text-chars walking the whole IR tree per estimate. Each block's char count is still summed across all its descendants. Future opt: cache char counts on IR nodes at parse time, or estimate block height in O(1) via stored attrs.
asi.next_action_hint: wire ir->lines-tail into virtual.clj when (and (= idx (dec n)) (nil? scroll)) - same gating as broken A2 wireup but using the SEMANTICALLY CORRECT tail walker. Layout reports true bubble height via estimated-height-with-turn-separator (already char-based, accurate). Painter renders prewrapped-lines at bottom-of-bubble offset (which it already does for any bubble shorter than its height contract). Need to verify painter behaviour or thread a 'this-is-a-tail' flag through.
asi.lesson: structural correctness is NOT optional - A2 had identical timing wins but was broken. A3 has the same wins AND preserves the user-visible contract because the algorithm matches what auto-scroll-to-bottom actually wants.
asi.limitation: tail-walker is correct for atomic blocks. If a single huge block (e.g. 100k-line code block with no inner structure) exceeds tail-budget, walker still emits that one block fully then takes-last tail-n. For the bench's lorem prose this never triggers because blocks are small.

description: layout baseline (no wireup): full O(body) walk for streaming bubble through project-message. 33.7ms in 50k-100k bucket, p99 47.7, linearity 34x. JVM warmed.

asi.hypothesis: lock honest layout-driven baseline before wiring tail walker
asi.context: stash includes virtual.clj wireup; running ca44be7c walker function exists but never called. JVM had ~10 prior bench runs; baseline now consistently 33-37ms across 5 settle runs.

description: A4: wire ir->lines-tail through virtual/layout for tail-pinned bubble (last msg + scroll==nil). 33.7ms->9.4ms (3.5x). Output bit-identical to (take-last N (full-walk)) (verified at REPL: tail-eq-last-of-full?=true, 80=last-80-of-1926). Scrolled-up unchanged at ~34ms (same-JVM cross-check).

asi.hypothesis: wire :tail-lines (* 2 inner-h) into virtual/project-message via virtual/layout when tail-pinned (idx==dec n AND scroll==nil); falls through to full render otherwise. Uses A3's ir->lines-tail back-walker to render correct content (last N lines, not first N).
asi.result: 3.5x speedup on layout-driven bench: 9.0/9.5/9.7ms vs 33.7ms baseline. Total wall 2270->679ms. p99 47.7->13.4ms. max 49.7->15.4ms.
asi.correctness: REPL trace: (= bubble-tail (take-last (count bubble-tail) bubble-full)) returns true. The 80 lines rendered tail-pinned are EXACTLY the last 80 of the 1926-line full render. Total-h reported as 86 (rendered count + chrome) so layout's auto-bottom math correctly positions viewport on the actual tail.
asi.non_regression: scrolled-up path (scroll != nil) bench in same JVM = 34ms == baseline. Wireup gates on (nil? scroll); when user scrolls, full-render fallback fires, identical to pre-wireup behaviour.
asi.scroll_up_during_streaming_caveat: scrolled-up + actively-streaming has every-chunk full render at ~34ms/frame. 80ms render throttle covers it but tight. Future iter: mid-window walker that emits only the rows currently visible (not just tail). For now this matches the original behaviour - we made auto-pinned faster without making scroll-up worse.
asi.lesson: structural correctness AND contract preservation. A2 (head-cap) had bench wins but broke auto-bottom semantics. A3+A4 (tail walk + gated wireup) keep the bench wins AND the user-visible contract. Critical: gate every fast path on the precise condition where it's correct, fall through otherwise.

description: A5: replace (char-count (extract-text ir)) with ir-char-count + identity-hash cache in estimated-height. Neutral - same-JVM side-by-side: A4-only 1.65ms median, A5 1.62ms median (5 runs each). The autoresearch run_experiment 10.4 reading is JVM-cold noise; clean A/B was indistinguishable.

asi.hypothesis: estimated-height calls extract-text on streaming bubble's IR every frame (no :text yet); replacing with a char-summing walker + identity-hash cache should skip the per-paragraph string concat + final char-count walk
asi.rollback_reason: no measurable improvement on the layout bench. In the bench, only ONE bubble (the streaming one) hits the estimated-height cold path each frame; height-cache-get covers the others. Saved extract-text cost is small relative to layout overhead.
asi.next_action_hint: this micro-cost matters in scenarios with MANY pre-projection bubbles (large scrollback, fresh resume of long conversation). Bench doesn't model that. Future bench mode: 'long-scrollback resume' that estimates many fresh-IR bubbles. Or focus on real bottleneck: scroll-up-during-streaming still pays full O(body) per chunk (~34ms) - mid-window walker would help.
asi.lesson: optimizations on the slow path of an already-fast common case don't show on a bench dominated by other paths. Need to either (a) match bench to optimization or (b) target paths that ARE in the hot loop.
asi.code_value: ir-char-count fn + cache could still be valuable as ir/extract-text replacement for callers that don't need strings - voice TTS, header chrome estimates. Not autoresearch-trackable but worth keeping in mind for a separate cleanup PR.

description: A5b: ir-char-count + identity-hash cache, validated on long-scrollback bench (30 msgs, cold-heights). 12.58ms vs 13.20ms baseline (5% trend, within ~2ms variance). Same-JVM A/B inconclusive. Discarding - not enough signal even on the realistic bench.

asi.hypothesis: long-scrollback bench (30 stable scrollback bubbles + 1 streaming, cold-heights every frame) would expose ir-char-count's benefit by forcing 30 estimated-height calls per frame in pass-1
asi.result: marginal ~5% trend (13.20->12.58 median across 5 runs each, same JVM) but variance is ~2ms so signal/noise < 1. Cannot claim a win.
asi.rollback_reason: even on the bench engineered to exercise the optimization, the savings are within noise floor
asi.why_no_bigger_win: ir-char-count cache hits after frame 1 for stable scrollback IRs; extract-text rebuilds every call but apparently isn't dominant either. Layout's other 30-msg pass-1 overhead (dispatch, cache lookups, cand-idx filter) likely dominates.
asi.non_overfitting_check: design correct: did add the realistic scenario (scrollback-size 30, cold-heights). Wasn't tweaking until I got numbers I wanted; honest A/B says neutral, log neutral.
asi.next_action_hint: real next target is mid-window walker for scroll-up-during-streaming (~34ms/frame, the only remaining non-trivial cost). The scrollback path is already fast enough.
asi.lesson: if an optimization is neutral on the bench DESIGNED to expose it, the optimization is probably not impactful. Stop arguing with the data.

description: scroll-up baseline: A4 gates tail-walker on (nil? scroll) only. scroll=999999 (clamped to bottom) bypasses fast path -> full O(body) render every frame. 33.4ms, p99 45.6, linearity 35x.

asi.hypothesis: lock honest scroll-up baseline before widening tail-walker gate
asi.context: scroll=999999 always clamps to max-scroll == est-tot - inner-h. eff-1 == max-scroll, so semantically identical to scroll=nil. But A4's gate (nil? scroll) skips fast path -> 35ms wasted per frame on full render.

description: A6: widen tail-walker gate from (nil? scroll) to (= eff-1 max-scroll). Bottom-locked scroll values clamp to same eff-1 as nil-scroll; semantically identical user-visible content. 33.4ms->9.85ms (3.4x, -70.5%). 3 runs: 9.47/10.34/9.73. p99 45.6->15.0. Correctness verified at REPL: scroll=nil and scroll=999999 produce identical prewrapped-lines; scroll=100 (mid) still gets full 1926-line render.

asi.hypothesis: A4 gated on (nil? scroll). But layout clamps scroll to max-scroll == (est-tot - inner-h) every frame, so any scroll value >= max-scroll produces identical eff-1 to nil-scroll - same viewport position, same correctness. Widening the gate to (= eff-1 max-scroll) covers Page-Down/End-key/wheel-past-bottom without breaking mid-scroll.
asi.result: 3.4x speedup on scroll-up bench: 9.47/10.34/9.73ms vs 33.4ms baseline. Total wall 2221->713ms. p99 45.6->15.0ms.
asi.correctness: REPL trace: scroll=nil (80 lines) == scroll=999999 (80 lines), nil-eq-clamp? = true. scroll=100 (mid-position) -> 1926 lines (full bubble), unchanged. The gate distinguishes 'effectively-at-bottom' from 'user-genuinely-scrolled-up'.
asi.non_regression: auto-bottom bench in same JVM 9.1-9.4ms = unchanged from A4 (was also ~9.4ms). The eff-1 calc is what mattered; we just removed the (nil? scroll) restriction.
asi.lesson: fast-path gates should match the SEMANTIC condition (eff-1 at bottom), not the SYNTACTIC trigger (scroll is nil). Layout already computes the right thing; A4 just under-used it.
asi.remaining_lever: the only remaining slow case is genuine mid-scroll OR top-scroll during streaming, where user explicitly looks at non-tail content. That still costs ~34ms/frame (full render). Mid-window walker would fix it but requires painter changes.

description: window-bench baseline (:full mode): full O(body) walk + (subvec lines 200 240) per frame. 20.65ms in 50k-100k bucket via autoresearch (post-reload-cold path). Same-JVM warm side-by-side showed 4.2ms steady; both numbers valid for the respective JIT-state regimes.

asi.hypothesis: lock window-bench baseline before measuring ir->lines-window
asi.context: bench script does :reload which flushes JIT state. autoresearch sees cold reading 20.65ms; same JVM warm steady is 4.19ms (5 warmup + 3 measure). The bench's ratio holds regardless of cold/warm regime.
asi.note: this metric measures the FUNCTION ir->lines-window in isolation, not end-to-end TUI. Wireup into virtual.clj would still need painter changes for actual window-positioning. Logged as infrastructure validation.

description: A7: ir->lines-window via :max-lines (+start +3*num) short-circuit + subvec [start, start+num). 20.65ms->4.14ms (5x autoresearch), 3 runs 4.09/4.12/4.22. Same-JVM warm side-by-side was 4.19->0.80 (5.2x). Output bit-identical to (subvec full start (+ start num)) verified at 40 (size×start×num) combinations.

asi.hypothesis: for genuine mid-scroll (user looking at non-tail content), no tail-walker can help. Window walker emits rows [start, start+num) by walking the body but bailing at start+num+slack via the A1 short-circuit, then subvec. Work is O(start+num), not O(body). The wider the bubble, the bigger the win.
asi.result: autoresearch 5x speedup (20.65->4.14ms, 3 consistent runs); same-JVM warm side-by-side 5.2x (4.19->0.80ms median).
asi.correctness: all-equal? = true at 40 combinations: 5 sizes (1k,5k,20k,50k,100k) × 4 starts (0,40,200,500) × 2 nums (40,80). When start>=body-length, returns []. Output bit-identical to (subvec (ir->lines body w) start (+ start num)).
asi.scope_note: this iter lands the FUNCTION as infrastructure, not the wireup. Wiring into virtual.clj for mid-scroll requires (a) painter changes to render prewrapped-lines at a window-offset within the bubble's allotted height, or (b) reporting bubble height = num lines so paint math collapses. The latter is what tail-walker (A4) already does for the tail case - same idea applies, just with start>0.
asi.next_action_hint: wire ir->lines-window into virtual/project-message when (a) this is the last message AND (b) scroll != nil AND (c) eff-1 != max-scroll (genuine mid-scroll). Pass {:window-start scroll-offset-within-bubble :window-num inner-h*2}. Layout's bubble-height-est knows scroll-within-bubble.
asi.limitation: for start near body bottom (e.g. start = 1450 in a 1500-line bubble), this saves little (still walks 1450+slack lines). The tail-walker A3 covers that case via back-walk.
asi.next_step: the function + bench validate the approach. The structural win lands in production when wired to virtual.clj (similar to A4 wireup of A3). That's the next iteration.

description: A8: ir-char-count + identity-hash cache replacing extract-text in estimated-height, re-tested at 500k body. Same JVM warm steady-state: baseline 6.9-7.2ms / A8 6.6-7.0ms in 100k+ bucket. Indistinguishable. The earlier "5x speedup" was JIT-warmup ordering: post-A8 runs had more warmup than pre-A8 runs.

asi.hypothesis: extract-text on 500k-char IR builds a 500k string just to count chars; ir-char-count + cache should dominate at large bodies
asi.result: neutral after proper warmup. Baseline 7.2/7.2/6.9 vs A8 7.0/6.9/6.6/6.7/6.7 in 100k+ bucket, same JVM, 5 runs each direction. Variance ~0.5ms, deltas <0.5ms.
asi.rollback_reason: no consistent improvement; previous 5x claim was JIT-warmup ordering artifact (A8 measurement came after more warmup than baseline)
asi.lesson_repeated: this is the SECOND time JIT warmup fooled me into thinking a marginal optimization was a big win. Pattern: optimization run on a hotter JVM than baseline. Defensive procedure for FUTURE: always run 5+ warmup runs in same JVM between A and B before measuring, and run B FIRST so any residual warmup advantage goes to A.
asi.real_floor_at_500k: warm steady-state floor is ~6-7ms per layout call at 500k body (100k+ bucket). 6ms ~= layout overhead (5 msgs pass-1 + project visible + height calc + visible-set assembly). text->ir cost at 500k is ~50ms but happens OUTSIDE bench timing (in production it would be per-chunk).
asi.next_action_hint: the in-bench layout cost is at architectural floor. Remaining wins are OUTSIDE the bench: (1) text->ir incrementalization (replace commonmark-java with append-aware parser), (2) actual TUI painter changes to consume A7 window output. Both are big work. Current state ships well: auto-bottom 1.6ms, scrolled-locked 9.85ms, mid-scroll uses A7 if wired.

description: production live-streaming baseline: progress->lines-data with 30 frozen iters + 1 growing live iter (100k chars target). 0.20ms 100k+ bucket, p99 0.42, max 6.96 (one outlier; otherwise sub-ms). Confirms the original user-reported "100k+ chars laggy" was NOT from this path - live streaming is well-optimized via existing live-thinking-preview-chars=800 tail-clip + per-iteration auto-collapse.

asi.hypothesis: verify the REAL production live-streaming hot path (progress->lines-data, fires during loading?=true) - my previous benches measured format-answer-markdown-data which is the COMPLETED bubble path, not the streaming path. Original user complaint was '100k+ chars in live streaming TUI is laggy'.
asi.result: live-streaming is FAST: 0.2ms mean, 0.42ms p99 even at 100k chars + 30 frozen iters. The render.clj path already has tail-clip (live-thinking-preview-chars=800) plus per-iteration auto-collapse at line/char thresholds. Buckets all 0.2-0.3ms.
asi.important_finding: the autoresearch A1/A3/A4/A6/A7 wins are real but for DIFFERENT paths: scrollback re-projection, resize, settings-toggle, scroll. NOT for live streaming itself. Live streaming was already well-tuned by prior work (live-thinking-preview-chars, viewport-rows handling, auto-collapse).
asi.regression_signal: if anyone removes live-thinking-preview-chars or the per-iteration auto-collapse, this metric will jump from 0.2ms to dozens of ms. Useful as a guard.
asi.open_question: where did the user's original lag at '100k+ chars in live streaming' actually come from? Hypotheses: (1) older version before tail-clip existed, (2) Lanterna painter / terminal cost not in our walker, (3) cumulative GC pressure in long sessions, (4) telemere logging per chunk, (5) core.async channel backpressure.
asi.next_action_hint: investigate the open question by adding TUI integration timing (around tui.clj's render loop, around screen.refresh()) rather than benching internals. The walker is provably fast; the lag must be elsewhere.

description: e2e baseline (render-live-bubble-frame! production fast-path): 46.6ms mean per frame on virtual terminal 200x50, 100k chars streaming. p99 65ms, max 68ms. BODY-SIZE INDEPENDENT (40-78ms across all buckets). The actual lag the user reported - chrome paint + Lanterna DELTA refresh, NOT the walker.

asi.hypothesis: the user's lag at '100k+ chars in live streaming' is NOT in the walker (we proved 0.2ms there) but in chrome paint + Lanterna refresh
asi.result: CONFIRMED. e2e bench via render-live-bubble-frame! against VirtualTerminal 200x50 shows mean 46.6ms, p99 65ms, max 68ms per frame. At the 80ms render throttle, p99 lands at 80% utilization - frames overflow under load. This matches the user's 'kinda laggy' complaint exactly.
asi.key_finding: the cost is BODY-SIZE INDEPENDENT: <5k = 77ms, 5k-20k = 78ms, 20k-50k = 63ms, 50k-100k = 64ms, 100k+ = 60ms. Walker is sub-ms. So the 50+ms per frame is chrome paint + Lanterna refresh, not content rendering. All previous autoresearch wins (A1/A3/A4/A6/A7) optimized paths that contribute <1ms.
asi.production_relevance: render-live-bubble-frame! fires every 80ms during streaming. It paints header, input box, footer, scrollbar, and the live bubble. For a 200x50 = 10000 cell terminal, even no-op cell writes through Lanterna's TextGraphics + DELTA refresh add up.
next_action_hints: audit Lanterna writes: skip TextGraphics.setCharacter when the cell value is unchanged from previous frame (Lanterna may already do this internally - need to verify)
redraw only the cells that changed by tracking dirty rows in the chrome (header/footer/input only change on user actions; should be skipped in 80ms ticks unless animating)
consider larger render throttle (100ms? 120ms?) - trades smoothness for budget headroom
profile Lanterna's refresh: which step (diff/encode/write) takes the bulk?
asi.lesson: always bench through the actual production code path. Pure-function benchmarks of the walker showed sub-ms cost, but that was 1% of the actual per-frame budget. The other 99% was in I/O glue we never measured.

description: A8: TTL cache (100ms) for current-goal in header.clj. Eliminates 2x SQLite query per render frame. 46.6ms->10.55ms mean (77% reduction), p99 65->29ms, max 68->32ms. Now well under the 80ms render throttle. Confirmed via 5 settle runs (11.1/11.6/10.4/11.9/10.7ms median).

asi.hypothesis: the 47ms e2e cost was hidden behind chrome paint. Phased profile revealed draw-header! = 21.7ms; further isolation showed current-goal alone = 12.56ms (a SQLite query). It's called TWICE per render frame (via header-rows + draw-header!). 2x12.5 = 25ms wasted per spinner tick.
asi.result: 77% reduction in e2e per-frame cost. 46.6 -> 10.55ms mean. 5 same-JVM runs all 10-12ms. p99 65 -> 29ms. max 68 -> 32ms. Now comfortably under 80ms throttle.
asi.fix_design: atom-based TTL cache: {conv-id [now-ms goal]} with 100ms TTL. 100ms is 1.25 spinner-ticks of slack so a goal mutation surfaces within 2 ticks worst-case. Goal extension itself can later push invalidations into this cache for truly instant updates.
asi.production_relevance: this is THE lag the original user complained about - 'when there is live streaming and there are like 100k+ chars in the fucking live streaming TUI it's kinda laggy'. The fix is body-size INDEPENDENT (header is the same regardless of bubble content); explains why all my walker optimizations didn't help the user-perceived lag.
asi.lesson: comments-vs-reality: header.clj had a comment claiming current-goal cost was 'measurably cheap (microseconds)'. Real cost was 12000x higher (12ms not us). Every 'cheap fast-path' claim deserves a benchmark to back it up. Auto-research caught this where code review never would.
asi.what_about_other_extensions: any other render-frame-called function that hits SQLite or external state is similarly suspect. Audit: footer.clj, header notification queue, status lookups. Future iteration.

description: A9: replace 8x str/index-of in sentinel-chunk? with single char-range scan. NEUTRAL/SLIGHT REGRESSION. Hand-rolled loop is slower than JVM's vectorized String.indexOf intrinsic. Same-JVM A/B: chat-bubble 2.55ms->3.30ms; e2e 10.55 vs 11.15 (within noise). The 46.60 baseline in autoresearch state is pre-A8 (goal cache), so this shows as "improvement" - misleading.

asi.hypothesis: sentinel-chunk? does 8x str/index-of on every chunk (~80 chars), 640 char-scans/chunk. Single char-range check would be 80 scans, 8x faster.
asi.result: REGRESSED slightly on micro-bench (chat-bubble 2.55->3.30ms), neutral on e2e. JVM intrinsic for String.indexOf is vectorized (AVX2/SSE char scan); hand-rolled .charAt+recur loop runs on JIT'd bytecode without the intrinsic.
asi.rollback_reason: no improvement; possible regression on per-paint micro-bench
asi.lesson: JVM intrinsics for String operations (indexOf, lastIndexOf, contains) are often faster than hand-rolled loops because they use SIMD. Don't out-clever the JIT on standard String search.
asi.next_action_hint: if we want to optimize sentinel-chunk?, the right move is `(< 0 (.length chunk))` cheap pre-check + a SINGLE String.indexOf call against a precomputed sentinel-character String. Or memoize the entire chunk's sentinel-ness in the chunk metadata produced upstream.
asi.remaining_work: after A8 fixed the goal-cache the e2e is at 10-11ms steady, p99 ~30ms - comfortably under 80ms throttle. Further wins require chunk-level changes upstream (don't even emit sentinels in chunks we won't paint), or Lanterna refresh internals (not our code).

description: A10: cache body-of-length results in ConcurrentHashMap to remove bench allocation overhead. NEUTRAL - progressive bench calls with size= chunk*(i+1), unique each frame, cache misses every call. Within noise (10.18 vs A8 10.55).

asi.hypothesis: the bench's body-of-length allocates a fresh StringBuilder + 100k chars per frame, adding ~5ms GC noise per frame to the bench. Caching by length would remove it.
asi.result: neutral - 9.8/10.0/10.3/11.2/11.1 same-JVM, same as A8 10.55 baseline. The bench's progressive loop uses size = chunk_chars * (i+1), so each frame has a UNIQUE size; cache misses every time. The cache was for a workload pattern the bench doesn't have.
asi.rollback_reason: no improvement (cache misses every frame in progressive workload), adds harmless but unnecessary code
asi.next_action_hint: to actually remove body-alloc overhead from the bench, refactor body-of-length to take size as a quantized bucket (e.g. round to nearest 5k) so multiple chunks hit the same cache entry. But the bench's design intent IS to hit fresh sizes - quantizing would change the workload. Better: bench an existing-cached-bubble re-render scenario.
asi.state_summary: after A8 (goal cache fix), production e2e cost is ~10-11ms mean, p99 ~30ms, max ~30ms. Well under the 80ms render throttle. Each component: layout 1.3ms (fresh body), chrome 0.6ms, chat-bubble 2.5ms, refresh 1.3ms, bench overhead ~5ms. The remaining production cost (~5-6ms in real production minus bench overhead) is Lanterna API + paint. Architectural floor reached.
